text:
Bayesian Networks (BNs) are widely used tools for reasoning under uncertainty in many different fields of science and are particularly suited for addressing complex intervention problems involving causality. A major challenge for BNs is that of learning the structure from data. This survey provides a technical review of the advances and the current status of the literature on learning the structure of Bayesian Networks. We list and comment on 74 different exact combinatorial algorithms that can be classified in three main families: constraint-based that use the conditional independence tests; score-based that optimize a score in order to find the structure; and hybrid methods that are designed to be more efficient and accurate.

This paper gives an informal yet rigorous introduction to many of the available causal discovery algorithms. Starting from the prototype algorithms PC and K2, we advance up to the currently most accurate exact and approximate methods and sketch the assumptions they need from the causal relationships modeled, such as causal faithfulness and causal sufficiency. In addition, we discuss evaluation of the performance of causal learners, consistency in performance statements in the causal learning literature, and ways to cope with a common source of causal learning imperfections: noisy data. Finally, we conclude with recommendations for users, and open challenges for causal learning researchers. This paper can be seen as the definitive reference on causal learning for researchers and implementors of causal learning algorithms.
